segments.title: How standard displacement could hide wartime weight
segments.summary: The Washington treaty's standard-displacement wording opens ways to understate ammunition, feed water, crew, and removable armour.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with the treaty phrase describing what a ship is intended to carry in war, showing how intention could diverge from the eventual wartime load. Ammunition, extra crew, feed water used as protection, and armour held ashore all become ways to build reserve capacity into a nominally compliant ship. The example explains why displacement figures were not a complete measure of combat capability.

evidence.note: The transcript reads the standard-displacement definition and applies it to ammunition, feed water, extra crew, and removable armour.

segments.title: Carrier definitions and the sub-10,000-ton escape hatch
segments.summary: Carrier rules leave room for wartime fittings, aviation stores outside declared weight, auxiliary conversions, and small carriers outside the cumulative tonnage cap.
segments.body: The carrier definition is tested against weapons and armour fitted only after mobilization, as well as aircraft personnel and stores that might be excluded from standard displacement. Dr. Clarke then sketches auxiliaries designed for rapid carrier conversion and notes that ships below 10,000 tons did not count against the treaty carrier allowance. That loophole could support small scouting carriers while preserving the limited tonnage for large fleet units.

evidence.note: The transcript examines carrier definitions, aviation-related weight, and the absence of an enforcement court.
The transcript discusses convertible auxiliaries and carriers below the 10,000-ton threshold.

segments.title: Cruisers designed for wartime gun and armour swaps
segments.summary: Treaty cruisers could use interchangeable turrets, shore-stored weapons, and armour prepared for rapid fitting after war began.
segments.body: The cruiser discussion turns the 8-inch gun ceiling into a modular-design problem. Barbettes and magazines could be sized for larger wartime turrets, while twin anti-aircraft mounts and armour waited in shore establishments during peace. Dr. Clarke also considers how ships marked for scrapping might linger as training or coast-defence assets rather than disappearing immediately.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes interchangeable cruiser turrets, adaptable magazines, retained hulls, and shore-stored anti-aircraft weapons.

segments.title: Oversized guns, reserve machinery, and G3 carrier conversions
segments.summary: Capital ships and fleet carriers gain hidden growth margins through gun liners, reserve machinery spaces, and creative use of conversion allowances.
segments.body: A nominal 16-inch gun could be built from a larger barrel with a thick liner, while turbo-electric propulsion would make later machinery additions easier than a conventional shaft arrangement. The carrier conversion clause could let Britain treat barely started G3 hulls as conversions and build large carriers with few inherited compromises. Combining those ships with sub-10,000-ton carriers would reserve the treaty allowance for a stronger fleet-carrier core.

evidence.note: The transcript covers gun liners, shore weapons, reserve machinery, and turbo-electric drive.
The transcript applies carrier conversion and tonnage rules to G3-derived ships and a mixed large-and-small carrier force.

segments.title: Cruiser carriers require an industrial system behind them
segments.summary: Small scouting carriers could support cruiser stations, but rapid wartime conversion also depends on cranes, dry docks, warehouses, and stored equipment.
segments.body: Rather than an overloaded Ryujo-style fleet carrier, the proposed sub-10,000-ton ship carries roughly 24 to 30 aircraft for scouting, air defence, and light attack alongside cruiser forces. Repeated construction would provide useful experimental decks and free aviation weight from cruisers. The wider scheme only works if navies also maintain the dockyard cranes, warehouses, dual-use dry docks, guns, and armour needed for rapid mobilization changes.

evidence.note: The transcript defines a small cruiser-carrier role and its likely air group and deployment pattern.
The transcript links repeated carrier construction and wartime refits to infrastructure, stores, cranes, and dry docks.

segments.title: British, American, and Japanese treaty-era design choices
segments.summary: The counterfactual compares British fast capital ships with the design penalties of Lexington's heavy guns and Kaga's slower battleship hull.
segments.body: Britain could exploit replacement and armour assumptions to field fast, lightly protected capital ships prepared for wartime strengthening. For the United States, removing Lexington's 8-inch batteries avoids severe topweight and frees tonnage when cruiser numbers were not yet capped. Japan's Kaga conversion shows why carrier speed and hull form mattered to aircraft operations, long-distance movement, and an attritional Pacific strategy.

evidence.note: The transcript outlines British F3-style capital ships and possible replacement construction.
The transcript compares Lexington armament choices with Akagi and Kaga hull speed and carrier operations.

segments.title: Why widespread cheating prevents a London Naval Treaty
segments.summary: Open exploitation of Washington Treaty gaps produces a competitive small-carrier and cruiser race instead of a credible London agreement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that the Royal Navy did consider a Hermes-derived carrier below 10,000 tons but feared prompting rivals to copy it. In this scenario Britain, the United States, Japan, France, and smaller navies instead develop cruiser or scout carriers, while shipbuilding and supporting infrastructure continue at higher rates. With more ships already in service and no trust in declared limits, a later London treaty becomes politically and strategically implausible.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses the rejected Hermes-derived concept and how several navies might adopt scout carriers.
The transcript connects expanded fleets and shipyard capacity to the failure of a later London treaty.

segments.title: Could stronger interwar fleets improve deterrence?
segments.summary: The closing argument asks whether less disarmament leaves Britain, France, and Japan confident enough to manage crises without encouraging aggression.
segments.body: The counterfactual's central strategic claim is that states with credible forces can back diplomatic warnings and avoid the insecurity created by deep disarmament. Dr. Clarke suggests stronger, better-balanced fleets might separate or deter later conflicts rather than automatically cause a new naval war. He treats the result as uncertain, especially across different European and Asian political settings, but argues that confidence and presence materially shape crisis management.

evidence.note: The transcript links stronger forces to deterrence, force balance, and the possibility of avoiding a single world war.
The transcript considers how confidence might affect Japan, France, Italy, Germany, and smaller carrier-building powers.
